current_fact.fact_text: In 1889, the Wham Paymaster robbery saw bandits ambush a U.S. Army pay wagon in Arizona Territory, stealing over $28,000 in gold and silver. Despite a dramatic shootout and later trial, no one was convicted, and two soldiers received Medals of Honor for defending the wagon—turning a failed pursuit into a story of gritty valor.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

current_fact.match_reason: A dusty frontier robbery full of tension and grit fits the "fog and fire" duality—smoky uncertainty mixed with bursts of fiery action, echoed in the bluesy, dramatic Italian songwriting.

queue.fact_text: On May 11, 2011, the Istanbul Convention was opened for signature in Istanbul, aiming to combat violence against women and domestic violence across Europe. It remains one of the most comprehensive international efforts to define, prevent, and prosecute gender-based violence as a human rights violation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: A landmark treaty against gender-based violence resonates strongly with an episode amplifying powerful women in hip-hop, including Queen Latifah’s anthem "U.N.I.T.Y." about respect and safety.

queue.fact_text: On May 11, 1970, an F5 tornado tore through Lubbock, Texas, killing 26 people and causing massive damage. The disaster led to one of the most detailed urban tornado studies ever conducted, shaping building codes and emergency planning far beyond the city itself.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: The violent chaos of an F5 tornado contrasts with a "Slow Planet" invitation to breathe and slow down, offering a sonic antidote to nature’s most frantic winds and a reminder of the calm we can design into our environments.

queue.fact_text: In 1894, about 4,000 workers at the Pullman Palace Car Company launched a wildcat strike over wage cuts without rent reductions in their company town. Their protest soon expanded into a nationwide rail boycott, becoming one of the most significant labor struggles in U.S. history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A massive labor strike against corporate power pairs naturally with an episode dedicated to a band whose entire catalogue is a soundtrack to workers’ rights, protest, and systemic critique.

queue.fact_text: On May 11, 1812, British Prime Minister Spencer Perceval was assassinated in the lobby of the House of Commons by a disgruntled merchant named John Bellingham. He remains the only British prime minister ever killed in office, a reminder that political power has always carried personal risk.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A sudden political assassination invites somber reflection on fragility and abrupt change, fitting with an introspective episode about unforeseen rupture and the slow work of recovery.

queue.fact_text: On May 11, 2014, the Burmese military executes at least 37 villagers during the Mon Taing Pin massacre in Sagaing, Myanmar.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The deep, shadowy textures of trip-hop have often scored stories of conflict, injustice, and uneasy calm; this cinematic, boundary-blurring episode suits a grim event that sits in the gray zones of power and violence.

queue.fact_text: On May 11, 2011, the Istanbul Convention is signed in Istanbul, Turkey.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: Connecting the convention to its host city, this episode’s Arabic melodies and medina atmospheres evoke the cultural world around Istanbul while its gradual crescendo hints at the slow, hopeful build of social change.

queue.fact_text: The 68th edition of the Eurovision Song Contest is held in Malmö, Sweden. Nemo from Switzerland wins with their song "The Code", making them the contest's first non-binary winner.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

queue.match_reason: Eurovision is Europe’s maximalist pop playground, and Nemo’s theatrical, electronic performance fits sonically and emotionally with an 80s-club journey through synths, queer-coded anthems, and dance-floor drama.

queue.fact_text: Space Shuttle Atlantis is launched on the final mission to service the Hubble Space Telescope.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Both Hubble servicing and Arctic whale dives are about entering extreme, hostile environments—space and icy oceans—to get closer to awe; the episode’s cinematic calm mirrors the quiet, meticulous wonder of orbiting above Earth to tune a telescope.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 2009, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on STS-125, the final mission to service the Hubble Space Telescope. Astronauts essentially gave Hubble a 'cosmic upgrade,' installing new instruments and repairing aging systems so it could keep rewriting our understanding of the universe for years after the shuttles retired.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A mission that extended Hubble’s life fits a 'Space Shift' theme about turning emptiness into creative space, with energetic tracks echoing the optimism of upgrading a space telescope instead of abandoning it.

published.fact_text: In May 2024, a series of powerful solar storms battered Earth's magnetosphere, producing auroras visible far closer to the equator than usual. These storms were the most intense since the 2003 "Halloween storms," a reminder that our star can quietly unleash planetary-scale space weather.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: Geomagnetic storms are literally electromagnetic 'shocks' from the Sun, so an episode built around electrifying guitar energy mirrors the raw charge of space weather hitting Earth’s field.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 2024, Switzerland’s Nemo won the Eurovision Song Contest with "The Code," becoming the competition’s first non-binary winner. Their genre-blending performance turned a pop stage into a celebration of identity, fluidity, and bending categories that once felt fixed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: Nemo’s victory was a landmark in unboxing gender and genre on a global stage, resonating with an episode explicitly about breaking labels and embracing who we are.

published.fact_text: In 1813, explorers William Lawson, Gregory Blaxland, and William Wentworth found a viable route across Australia’s formidable Blue Mountains. Their crossing broke what settlers called the "impassable" barrier, opening the continent’s vast inland plains to colonial expansion—for better and for worse.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A story of crossing intimidating terrain and charting new routes pairs well with a seafaring, journey-focused episode where travel, resilience, and companionship shape the adventure.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 2011, a magnitude 5.1 earthquake struck Lorca, Spain, causing surprising damage and killing nine people, despite its moderate strength. The tragedy highlighted how shallow quakes and vulnerable historic buildings can turn a brief tremor into a deep scar on a city’s memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: An earthquake that hit a community unexpectedly fits an episode about surviving storms and trusting that devastation and fear are chapters—powerful, but not the whole book.

published.fact_text: In 1919, Uruguay became a signatory to the Buenos Aires Convention, an early treaty harmonizing copyright protection across the Americas. It helped formalize the idea that songs, books, and artworks are not just culture, but creations with rights that travel beyond borders.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A treaty about protecting creative works aligns with an episode meditating on music as something finite and precious, inviting respect for the artists behind the sounds.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 1068, Matilda of Flanders was crowned Queen of England alongside William the Conqueror, becoming a powerful political actor in her own right. While William waged campaigns, Matilda governed in his stead, showing that medieval queenship could mean ruling, not just standing beside a crown.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: Celebrating a medieval queen who wielded real power fits with a tribute to powerful women’s voices that reshape how we listen and who we consider central to the story.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 1998, India conducted three underground nuclear tests at Pokhran, surprising much of the world. The blasts, codenamed "Operation Shakti," announced India’s arrival as a declared nuclear power and sparked fierce debate about security, deterrence, and the dangers of atomic pride.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A controversial nuclear test that shifted geopolitical tensions sits well with an episode centered on politically charged music that questions power, militarism, and control.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 1997, IBM’s Deep Blue defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov in their final rematch game, the first time a computer beat a reigning champion in a classic match. It was a symbolic turning point, when silicon calculation met human intuition—and won.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A landmark human–machine showdown lines up with an episode on our psychological entanglement with technology and computers, including a track literally titled "Computer Love."

published.fact_text: On May 11, 1987, former Gestapo officer Klaus Barbie went on trial in Lyon for crimes against humanity committed during World War II, including the torture of resistance members and deportation of Jewish children. Decades after the war, France forced a reckoning with atrocities that many survivors had carried in silence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A late war-crimes trial is about societies slowly learning to confront their own history; pairing it with an episode on difficult, long-term learning underscores that painful progress can still be progress.

published.fact_text: In 973, Edgar the Peaceful was crowned King of England in the first known formal English coronation ceremony, though he had ruled for years already. The ritual—including the coronation of his wife Ælfthryth—helped invent the pageantry and symbolism that still echo in modern royal coronations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

published.match_reason: An early English coronation that codified royal ritual mirrors an episode about a band who deliberately treats performances like elaborate ceremonies, all robes, masks, and mythology.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 2014, a soccer match at Kinshasa’s Stade Tata Raphaël turned deadly when police fired tear gas into the stands, triggering a stampede that killed 15 people. A space meant for collective joy became, in moments, a stark lesson in how crowd dynamics and policing can turn dangerous.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

published.match_reason: A stadium disaster at a sports event evokes questions about how we care for crowded communal spaces, mirroring an episode that reflects on clubs and dancefloors as spaces that should be joyful, safe, and respectful.

published.fact_text: In 1560, the Ottoman Navy decisively defeated a Spanish-led fleet at the Battle of Djerba off the North African coast. It marked the zenith of Ottoman naval power in the Mediterranean, a sea that had become a crossroads of empires, trade routes, and shifting cultural tides.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A pivotal naval clash for control of the Mediterranean pairs naturally with a musical 'boat' journey across the same sea, letting listeners feel its many coasts rather than its conflicts.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 2022, the Burmese military carried out the Mon Taing Pin massacre in Sagaing Region, executing at least 37 villagers, including children. The attack became one of the starkest symbols of escalating brutality in Myanmar’s post-coup civil conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A brutal massacre in a struggle against military rule calls for a politically conscious soundscape; Residente’s work lives precisely at the intersection of art, resistance, and naming violence.

published.fact_text: On May 11, 1996, ValuJet Flight 592 crashed into Florida’s Everglades after a fire, sparked by improperly stored chemical oxygen generators, broke out in the cargo hold. The disaster, which killed all 110 aboard, exposed how invisible technical decisions and cost-cutting can have catastrophic consequences.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A tragic flight that should have been a routine journey contrasts with an episode about mindful, intentional travel, inviting listeners to consider how movement can be handled with care rather than reckless haste.

published.fact_text: In 1889, the Wham Paymaster robbery saw bandits ambush a U.S. Army pay wagon in Arizona Territory, stealing over $28,000 in gold and silver. Despite a dramatic shootout and later trial, no one was convicted, and two soldiers received Medals of Honor for defending the wagon—turning a failed pursuit into a story of gritty valor.
